naturalMeaning: Plural 'días' even for one morning. Switches to 'buenas tardes' after noon, 'buenas noches' after dark.
Both a greeting when arriving at night and a farewell going to bed — context decides.
In Mexico people more often say 'bye' (yes, the English word), 'nos vemos', or 'ahí nos vemos' casually.
'Muchas gracias' / 'mil gracias' for more warmth. Reply: 'de nada' or the very Mexican 'por nada'.

examples.translation: Thank you very much. — You're welcome.

naturalMeaning: 'Por nada' is the equally common Mexican variant; 'no hay de qué' is a touch more polite.
For bumping into someone or a small apology. 'Con permiso' is for passing THROUGH; 'disculpe' for getting attention.
The usted form, for flagging a stranger or a waiter. 'Disculpa' is the tú version.

pronunciationNotes: The accent distinguishes 'sí' (yes) from 'si' (if) — same sound, different word.

naturalMeaning: Also the negator placed before the verb: 'no hablo español'.
Abbreviated 'Sr.'. 'Señora' (Sra.) for married/older women, 'señorita' (Srta.) for young women — though the last is fading.
Drops to 'un' before a masculine noun (un café) and becomes 'una' before a feminine one (una cerveza).

pronunciationNotes: Seseo: the c before i is [s], not the Spanish [θ]. 'SEEN-ko'.

naturalMeaning: 'Cien' is exactly 100; it becomes 'ciento' in 101-199 (ciento uno).
Prices: 'mil pesos' = 1,000 pesos. Never takes -s for the number itself.
Masculine despite the -a ending: el día, buenos días.
Age uses 'tener': 'tengo treinta años'. Watch the ñ — 'ano' without it means anus.
Two meanings: 'mañana' = tomorrow, 'la mañana' = the morning. 'Por la mañana' = in the morning.
'La tarde' = afternoon/early evening; as an adverb 'tarde' = late. 'Buenas tardes' greets from noon on.

examples.translation: Yesterday I ate at a restaurant.

naturalMeaning: 'Ahora' = now; 'ahorita' = the famously elastic Mexican 'right now / in a bit / eventually' — see the slang notes.

examples.text: Siempre tomo café en la mañana.
examples.translation: I always have coffee in the morning.

naturalMeaning: Days aren't capitalized. 'El lunes' = on Monday; 'los lunes' = on Mondays. Same form singular and plural (el lunes / los lunes).
Sunday is family day — the big midday 'comida', often at grandma's.
Stress on the last syllable: ma-MÁ. 'Madre' is the formal word; day to day it's 'mamá' or 'amá'.
pa-PÁ. Careful with stress: 'papa' (no accent) is potato, and 'el Papa' is the Pope.
'Hermana' = sister; 'hermanos' can mean brothers OR siblings in general. Silent h.
'Hija' = daughter; 'hijos' = children. The j is the throaty [x] sound; silent h.
'Esposa' = wife. Casual alternatives: mi viejo/vieja, mi señor/señora.
'Amiga' for a female friend. Very casual Mexican synonyms: cuate, compa, carnal.
Also a filler exclamation among friends: '¡Ándale, hombre!' Silent h.
'Mi mujer' commonly means 'my wife' in everyday speech.
Grammatically singular: 'la gente es amable' (people are friendly). The g before e is the [x] sound.
'Niña' = girl. Mexican casual for kids: chavos, morros, escuincles.
Feminine but takes 'el' in the singular for sound (el agua) — still feminine: 'el agua fría'. 'Agua de jamaica/horchata' are the classic aguas frescas.

examples.translation: Can I get a water, please?

naturalMeaning: The drink, the place, and the color brown all in one word: 'ojos café' = brown eyes.

examples.text: Un café con leche, por favor.

naturalMeaning: 'Pan dulce' (sweet bread — conchas, etc.) is a whole Mexican institution; 'pan blanco' is sandwich bread.
In Mexico, corn tortillas by default — the staple carb. (In Spain 'tortilla' is an omelet; not here.)

examples.translation: Can I get some tortillas, please?

naturalMeaning: The national food. 'Echarse un taco' loosely = grab a bite. Order by filling: al pastor, de bistec, de suadero.

examples.text: Dos tacos al pastor, por favor.

naturalMeaning: Central to Mexican food; '¿pica?' (is it spicy?) is a survival question. Also slang for penis — context is everything.

examples.translation: Does the salsa have chili in it?

naturalMeaning: 'Carne asada' — grilled meat — is the weekend cookout centerpiece, like a Mexican barbecue.
'Pescado' is fish on the plate; 'pez' is the live animal.
Street fruit cups ('fruta con chile y limón') are everywhere.
Usually plural for 'vegetables': las verduras.
Casual: 'una chela'. A michelada is beer with lime, salt, and chili. Seseo: both c and z are [s].

examples.text: Una cerveza bien fría, por favor.

naturalMeaning: Verb 'desayunar'. Chilaquiles, huevos a la mexicana, tamales — Mexican breakfast is a serious meal.
Two meanings: food in general, and specifically 'la comida' = the big 2-4 p.m. meal, the main one of the day in Mexico.

examples.translation: Have you eaten? Lunch is ready.

naturalMeaning: Verb 'cenar'. Lighter and later than lunch — often just tacos, pan dulce, or a torta.
'La cuenta, por favor' asks for the check. 'Cuenta' is also a bank account.

examples.translation: Can you bring us the check, please?

naturalMeaning: 'En casa' = at home; 'a casa' = homeward. 'Está usted en su casa' is a warm Mexican welcome.

examples.text: Me voy a mi casa después del trabajo.

naturalMeaning: Mexican word for apartment (Spain uses 'piso'). Often shortened to 'depa'.
A room, especially a bedroom (also 'recámara'/'habitación'). Also 'a quarter': 'un cuarto de hora'.
'¿Dónde está el baño?' is essential. In public places may be labeled 'sanitarios' or 'WC'.
'La Ciudad de México' (CDMX) or just 'el D.F.' colloquially. Seseo on both the c and the final d is soft/dropped.
The covered neighborhood market. The weekly open-air version is a 'tianguis' — a Nahuatl word.

examples.text: Voy al mercado por fruta y verdura.
examples.translation: I'm going to the market for fruit and vegetables.

naturalMeaning: The tiny corner store is 'la tiendita'. The big convenience chain is the OXXO (say 'el ocso').
The very Mexican informal word is 'chamba' (job/gig). The j is the [x] sound.

examples.text: Voy al trabajo a las ocho.
examples.translation: I go to work at eight.

naturalMeaning: Mexican slang: 'lana', 'varo', 'feria' (small change). 'No traigo dinero' = I don't have money on me.

examples.translation: I don't have cash on me.

naturalMeaning: The currency. Slang 'un varo' = one peso / money generally. Also literally 'weight'.

examples.translation: I have something to tell you.

naturalMeaning: Identity, origin, profession, time, permanent traits: soy, eres, es, somos, son. Contrast with 'estar' for states and location.
Temporary states, location, feelings: estoy, estás, está, estamos, están. '¿Cómo estás?' 'Está en la casa.'
Possession AND states English does with 'to be': tener hambre (be hungry), tener años (be … old), tener frío (be cold). Forms: tengo, tienes, tiene, tenemos, tienen.
Wildly irregular: voy, vas, va, vamos, van. 'Ir a + infinitive' is the everyday future: 'voy a comer'. '¡Vámonos!' = let's go!

examples.translation: I'm going to the beach tomorrow.

naturalMeaning: Also weather: 'hace calor/frío'. Silent h; irregular yo form 'hago'. '¿Qué haces?' = what are you doing?

examples.translation: What are you doing this weekend?

naturalMeaning: Irregular: digo, dices, dice, decimos, dicen. '¿Cómo se dice…?' = how do you say…? 'Mande' is the Mexican 'come again?'

examples.translation: How do you say 'receipt' in Spanish?

naturalMeaning: Stem-changing: puedo, puedes, puede, podemos, pueden. '¿Puedo…?' asks permission.
Stem-changing: quiero, quieres, quiere, queremos, quieren. 'Te quiero' = I love you (warm, for family/friends/partners). Polite ordering: 'quisiera'.
For facts and skills; 'conocer' for people/places. Irregular yo: 'sé'. 'No sé' = I don't know.

examples.translation: I don't know where it is.
Do you know how to swim?

naturalMeaning: 'Nos vemos' = see you (the standard goodbye). 'A ver' = let's see. Also 'to watch': ver la tele.
The model regular -ar verb. Silent h. Also 'to call on the phone': 'te hablo al rato'.
The model regular -er verb. '¿Ya comiste?' (did you eat yet?) is practically a greeting.
'Tomar' is actually more common than 'beber' in Mexico for drinking: '¿quieres tomar algo?'

translation: to take; to drink; to have (food/drink)
naturalMeaning: Hugely versatile in Mexico: tomar un café (have a coffee), tomar el metro (take the metro), tomar (to drink alcohol). '¿Vamos a tomar algo?'

examples.translation: I'm going to have a coffee.

naturalMeaning: The model regular -ir verb: vivo, vives, vive, vivimos, viven.

examples.text: Vivo en la Ciudad de México.

naturalMeaning: Casual Mexican synonym: 'chambear'.
Works backwards from English: 'me gusta el café' = coffee is pleasing to me. The thing liked is the subject; see the grammar point.

examples.text: Me gusta mucho la comida mexicana.

naturalMeaning: The stronger 'gustar': 'me encanta' = I love it. Also 'encantado/a' = nice to meet you.

examples.translation: I need to go to the bathroom.
I want to buy some tortillas.

naturalMeaning: 'Me llamo…' = my name is (lit. I call myself). Also to phone: 'te llamo al rato'. Yeísmo: 'ya-MAR'.

examples.translation: My name is Ana. And you?

naturalMeaning: Stem-changing: entiendo, entiendes… 'No entiendo' is essential. Synonym 'comprender'.

examples.translation: I don't understand, can you repeat?

naturalMeaning: Feminine 'buena'; shortens to 'buen' before a masculine noun (buen día). '¿Bueno?' is also how Mexicans answer the phone.
Feminine 'mala'; shortens to 'mal' before a masculine noun. 'Estar malo' = to be sick.
Same form for both genders. Before a singular noun it shortens to 'gran' and means 'great': 'un gran día'.
Adjective 'small', or a noun for a young man/kid. 'Pequeño' is the more neutral 'small'.
Feminine 'nueva'. '¿Qué hay de nuevo?' = what's new?
Feminine 'vieja'. Affectionately, 'mi viejo/vieja' = my husband/wife or my dad/mom.
For object/food temperature. For weather use 'hace calor'; for a person 'tengo calor'. Careful: 'estar caliente' about a person is sexual slang.
'Tengo frío' = I'm cold; 'hace frío' = it's cold out; 'está frío' = it (the object) is cold.
Opposite 'barato' (cheap). 'Está muy caro' = it's too expensive.

examples.text: Está muy caro, ¿no tiene más barato?
examples.translation: That's too expensive, don't you have something cheaper?

naturalMeaning: With estar (temporary state): 'estoy cansado'. Feminine 'cansada'.
With estar: 'estoy contento'. 'Feliz' is deeper/more permanent happiness.

examples.translation: I'm really glad to see you.

examples.text: El español no es tan fácil.

naturalMeaning: Feminine 'bonita'. '¡Qué bonito!' = how nice! Common everywhere in Mexico.
Accent on question words: 'dónde' (where?) vs 'donde' (where, relative).
'¿Cómo?' alone = 'what?/pardon?' (polite). '¿Cómo estás?' = how are you? Without accent 'como' = like/as, or 'I eat'.
Two words with accent for the question '¿por qué?'; one word 'porque' (no accent) is the answer 'because'.
'¿Qué?' = what? '¿Qué onda?' = what's up (very Mexican). '¡Qué padre!' = how cool! Without accent 'que' = that/which.
Agrees: cuánto/cuánta/cuántos/cuántas. '¿Cuánto cuesta?' = how much does it cost?
'Acá' is a slightly looser 'over here', very common in Mexico.
'Allí/ahí' = there (more precise), 'allá' = over there (vaguer). Yeísmo: 'a-YA'.
'Muy' before adjectives/adverbs (muy bueno); 'mucho' with nouns/verbs (mucho dinero, comer mucho).
Agrees as an adjective: mucho/mucha/muchos/muchas. 'Muchas gracias', 'mucho gusto'.

examples.translation: There are a lot of people.

naturalMeaning: The negative counterpart is 'tampoco' (neither): 'yo tampoco'.
Synonyms: 'quizá(s)', and the very Mexican 'a lo mejor'.
'Conmigo' = with me, 'contigo' = with you (special fused forms).
Purpose, destination, deadline. Contrasts with 'por' — see the grammar point. '¿Para llevar?' = to go?
One word, no accent — the answer. Don't confuse with '¿por qué?' (why?).

examples.translation: I'm not going because I'm tired.

naturalMeaning: 'Nombre' = first name; 'apellido' = last name. On forms both appear.
Lowercase for the language. In Mexico people say they speak 'español'; 'castellano' is heard more in South America and Spain.
The x is the [x] sound (like j): 'MEH-hee-ko', never 'meksiko'. Same for 'mexicano', 'Oaxaca'.

pronunciationNotes: The double rr is the strong trill — contrast 'perro' (dog) with 'pero' (but), a classic minimal pair.

examples.text: Estoy leyendo un libro en español.
examples.translation: I'm reading a book in Spanish.

examples.text: Una mesa para dos, por favor.

naturalMeaning: The mobile is 'el celular' (or just 'el cel') in Mexico — not 'el móvil' as in Spain.
'Carro' is even more common in Mexico than 'coche'; both are understood everywhere.

examples.translation: Shall we go by car or metro?

naturalMeaning: In most of Mexico 'el camión' is the city bus — not 'autobús' (though that's understood). Elsewhere it means truck.

examples.translation: I'm going to take the bus.

naturalMeaning: Mexico City's metro is vast and cheap; stations have picture icons, useful when reading is hard.

examples.text: El metro está lleno en la mañana.
examples.translation: The metro is packed in the morning.

examples.text: Vamos a la playa el fin de semana.
examples.translation: We're going to the beach this weekend.

naturalMeaning: 'Hace sol' = it's sunny; 'hay mucho sol' too.
The verb is 'llover': 'está lloviendo' / 'llueve'. Yeísmo: 'YU-bya'.
Both meanings: 'no tengo tiempo' (no time) and '¿qué tiempo hace?' (what's the weather?). For clock time, use 'hora'.

examples.text: Los niños van a la escuela.

naturalMeaning: Verb 'preguntar' (to ask). Don't confuse with 'pregunta' the noun. 'Tengo una pregunta.'
Seseo: the z is [s], 'a-SU-kar'.
Also the tú command of 'salir' (leave!) — context tells them apart.

examples.translation: Can you pass me the salt?

naturalMeaning: Silent h + we = 'WE-bo'. Huevos rancheros, a la mexicana — breakfast staples. Note: 'huevos' is also crude slang for testicles, so it's a mild double-entendre.
Mexican red rice ('arroz rojo') is the default side. Seseo: final z is [s].
Usually plural: 'los frijoles'. Refried ('refritos') or de la olla — a staple at nearly every meal.

examples.text: Unos tacos de frijoles, por favor.

naturalMeaning: '¿Verde o roja?' (green or red?) is the classic taco-stand question. '¿Pica?' asks how spicy.

examples.translation: Is the green salsa very spicy?

naturalMeaning: In Mexico 'limón' is the small green lime you squeeze on everything. Squeezed on tacos, soup, beer, fruit — everything.
Same form both genders. Also 'un chiste verde' = a dirty joke.
Feminine 'blanca'.
Feminine 'negra'. 'Café negro' = black coffee.
Same form both genders. Seseo: 'a-SUL'.
The comparative word: 'más grande' (bigger), 'el más grande' (the biggest). 'Más o menos' = so-so. Accent distinguishes it from 'mas' (but, literary).
'Menos que' (less than); for clock time 'las diez menos cuarto' = quarter to ten. 'Más o menos' = kind of.
'Un poco' = a little; 'poquito' (diminutive) is extremely common and softens it further.
The answer to '¿cómo estás?': 'bien, gracias'. Also intensifies: 'bien frío' (nice and cold), 'está bien' (it's OK/alright).
'Me siento mal' = I feel unwell/bad. 'No está mal' = not bad.
'Todo el mundo' = everyone (lit. all the world).
'En lugar de' = instead of. 'Un buen lugar para comer' = a good place to eat.

examples.text: Conozco un buen lugar para comer.
examples.translation: I know a good place to eat.

naturalMeaning: Distinctively Mexican: the polite reply when someone calls your name or you didn't hear — where others say '¿qué?' or '¿cómo?'. Also answering a call.
Mexican word for a ticket (bus, plane, cinema). Spain uses 'billete'.

examples.text: Un boleto a Puebla, por favor.
